claim_text: Although there is a precedent for this in other radiologist reader studies,6,40 radiologist perceptions of image quality may not be directly correlated with true diagnostic accuracy.

sub_claims.text: Mason et al. 2020 is a precedent radiologist reader study of MR image quality.

evidence.snippet: In this study, we compare the image quality scores of five radiologists with the RMSE, SSIM, and other potentially useful IQMs... The comparison uses a database of MR images of the brain and abdomen that have been retrospectively degraded by noise, blurring, undersampling, motion, and wavelet compression for a total of 414 degraded images. A total of 1017 subjective scores were assigned by five radiologists.
This study investigates correlations between RMSE, SSIM, and eight other common objective IQMs with radiologists' scoring of diagnostic MR image quality.

Three body radiologists and two neuro radiologists were involved in the study. The radiologists scored only images from their subspecialties, using a 1-5 Likert scale. The radiologists were asked to rate the diagnostic quality of the images with respect to delineation of relevant anatomy and ability to confidently exclude a lesion on a 5-point scale.
We measured the correlations between 10 full-reference objective IQMs and the subjective image quality score of five subspecialty radiologists.

sub_claims.nuance: Mason et al. 2020 is explicitly a reader study in which five subspecialty radiologists subjectively scored the diagnostic quality of 414 degraded MR images (brain and abdomen) on a 1-5 Likert scale, establishing it as a precedent radiologist reader study of MR image quality. The manuscript cites mason2020 as one of two precedent reader-study references (the other being ref 40); the companion clause about radiologist perceptions not directly correlating with diagnostic accuracy is a separate authorial caveat, not a claim about mason2020's findings.

phrasings_tried: radiologist reader study of MR image quality
radiologists scoring diagnostic quality of MR images / subjective radiologist ratings
expert radiologists evaluating MR image quality (reader study design)
Likert-scale scoring of MR image quality by radiologists
attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Mason et al. is itself the primary reader study; it cites prior studies (Renieblas et al. [20], non-expert rater studies [16], [17]) but the reader study described is Mason's own work.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The manuscript cites Mason as a precedent for a radiologist reader study of MR image quality. Mason 2020 is precisely that kind of study — five radiologists rated 414 degraded MR images for diagnostic quality. Context matches.
